title-group.article-title: Risk of total knee replacement after a meniscal tear treated with arthroscopic partial meniscectomy compared to nonoperative treatment: A systematic review

contrib-group.aff: aBrigham and Women's Hospital, Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Boston, MA, USA
bHarvard Medical School, Boston, MA, USA
cMassachusetts General Hospital, Department of Internal Medicine, Boston, MA, USA

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Corresponding author. Orthopaedic and Arthritis Center for Outcomes Research (OrACORe) Brigham and Women's Hospital, 75 Francis Street, BTM 5-016, Boston, MA, USA. jnkatz@bwh.harvard.edu

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: Knee Osteoarthritis (OA) is a leading cause of disability worldwide. Many of those with OA have imaging evidence of a meniscal tear which can be treated with arthroscopic partial meniscectomy (APM) or nonoperatively. If symptoms persist after meniscal tear treatment, a total knee arthroplasty (TKA) is often offered if patients have concomitant advanced OA. We sought to assess differences in rates of TKA after five years in patients with meniscal tear who were treated with APM vs nonoperative treatments using existing literature.

sec.title: Design
sec.p: Using the PubMed database, we performed a systematic literature review to identify papers comparing eventual TKA rates in patients with meniscal tear treated with APM vs nonoperative management. We abstracted pertinent information on study design and patient population. We extracted five-year TKA rates for operative and nonoperative groups using reported values or estimates derived from provided Kaplan-Meier (KM) curves.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Our search returned seven studies, which varied in design, sample size, and primary outcome. Some studies were restricted to root tears, which are associated with higher rates of progression to TKA. Five-year TKA incidence ranged from 2.2% to 54.0% in the APM group and 2.9%–34.6% in the nonoperative group. All but one paper indicated that those in the APM group progressed to TKA at higher rates than those in the nonoperative group.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Prior surgical interventions for meniscal tear may increase incidence of future TKA compared to nonoperative intervention. These findings may prompt further investigation into the relationship between APM and incidence of TKA.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Knee osteoarthritis (OA) is a leading cause of pain, loss of function, and disability in adults worldwide [1]. It is estimated to affect over 14 million individuals in the US [2] and account for $65 billion [3] in direct medical costs annually. Meniscal damage is often observed on MRI in individuals with knee OA [4]. Symptomatic meniscal tears are generally treated nonoperatively with combinations of exercise, physical therapy, and medication [5]. If these first line treatments do not work, meniscal tears are often treated surgically with arthroscopic partial meniscectomy (APM) [5]. While MRI and APM are commonly used in clinical contexts [6,7], most contemporary guidelines discourage their routine use for degenerative knee disease [8,9]. Instead, current recommendations emphasize initial evaluation with clinical assessment and weight-bearing radiography, reserving advanced imaging and surgical intervention for specific clinical indications [8,9].

Randomized controlled trials conducted over the last 15 years have suggested that middle-age and older individuals with meniscal tear and knee pain have similar long-term pain relief and improvement in functional status following APM as they do following nonoperative management [10]. Additionally, a Cochrane systematic review from O'Connor et al. demonstrated high certainty evidence that APM provided little to no difference in pain or function in the three months following surgery [11]. Total knee arthroplasty (TKA) is generally offered to patients suffering from advanced knee OA after other treatments have failed to relieve symptoms [12].

To our knowledge, no prior studies have systematically reviewed differences in eventual TKA rates between subjects treated with APM and those treated nonoperatively for meniscal tear. This systematic literature review aims to fill that gap by investigating reported risk of subsequent TKA in studies that compared operative and nonoperative treatment of persons with knee pain and meniscal damage.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Search: We searched the PubMed database to identify articles related to meniscal tear and total knee arthroplasty. The search algorithm was: {Meniscectomy OR Partial Meniscectomy OR APM} AND {TKR OR TKA OR Total Knee Replacement OR Arthroplasty}. We sought papers that compared rates of subsequent TKA between subjects with meniscal tear who received either conservative care or APM. The PubMed database was last searched January 31, 2025.

Exclusion: Papers were excluded using the following exclusion criteria: written in a language other than English, published before 2010, study not conducted on human subjects, case study, focus on condition other than meniscal tear, focus on surgical technique, laboratory science investigation, study reported no data on risk of total knee arthroplasty, opinion articles, articles that did not compare both surgical and non-surgical treatments, or modeled data. We first screened papers by title and abstract and removed papers that met one or more exclusion criteria. The remaining articles were reviewed in full and removed if they met any of the aforementioned exclusions. We then reviewed the references of the eligible studies to identify additional papers that fit our inclusion criteria and did not meet any exclusion criteria [Fig. 1]. Two reviewers independently screened each record, and both screens yielded the same seven papers. To evaluate study quality, we conducted critical appraisals for all papers using Joanna Briggs Institute’s critical appraisal tools [13]. We selected JBI's critical appraisal tools because they include a range of checklists that are suitable for various study designs.Fig. 1
Systematic review process.Fig. 1

Data Abstraction: We abstracted from eligible papers the following information: author, year published, country of study, design, and data source [Table 1]. We also collected relevant participant characteristics including sample period, number of subjects receiving APM, number of subjects receiving nonoperative treatment, mean age, gender distribution, and method of ascertainment of TKA and APM (e.g. codes from administrative data, review of medical record) [Table 2]. We then extracted information about the percentage of the study population who received a TKA at 5 years, main study findings, and main study limitations [Table 3]. We separated papers based on whether they were restricted to root tears. Two reviewers independently extracted study characteristics and results. Any conflicts were resolved through discussion.Table 1
Basic paper and study characteristics.Table 1

Found increase in TKA rate in patients treated with APM compared to non-operative management•

Papers not restricted to root tears
Barnds et al.∼20%a∼15%a•

Hernigou et al.Overall19.2% (24/125)Age 60-6916.7% (15/90)Age >7025.7% (9/35)Overall11.6% (8/69)Age 60-6915.6% (7/45)Age >704.2% (1/24)•

Katz et al.ITT∼12%aAT ∼12%aITT∼6%aAT ∼3%a•

Noorduyn et al.ITT2.2% (3/139)ITT2.9% (4/139)•

13.6% did not complete 5-year follow-up survey which was used to identify subsequent TKA, resulting in a risk of biased loss of follow-up•
Insufficient data provided to perform AT analysis
Park et al.∼3.5%a∼3.0%a•
Found higher TKA rate at 5 and 10 years (17% and 25% relative increase respectively) in APM group compared to non-op group•
Among patients receiving 2nd TKA, average time to TKA was 5.9 years for APM group and 5.5 years for non-op group•
Cannot determine whether TKA was done on the same side of APM•
Used second TKR as the primary outcome which may lead to conservative estimates•
TKA rate at 5 years was difficult to estimate based on how KM curve was presented in the paper
ITT: Intention to treat, AT: As Treated.a
Estimated from Kaplan-Meier curves presented in the paper.

Analysis: The goal of our analysis was to estimate the cumulative incidence of TKA in each treatment group at five years. We chose five years because each paper reported information on cumulative TKA incidence at or beyond five years. Papers either gave a count of people who had a TKA at the five-year time-point (permitting us to calculate a percentage of those followed in the APM and the nonoperative cohorts) or provided a Kaplan-Meier (KM) curve. For the papers that provided a KM curve, we used the curve to estimate visually the percentage of people who had received a TKA at five years.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Search results: Fig. 1 demonstrates the results of data extraction and paper review at each screening stage. The search terms identified 628 full articles. We excluded 207 articles that were not in English, were published before 2010, or did not study human subjects. We screened the remaining 421 papers by title and abstract. This screening process removed 372 articles that met at least one of the exclusion criteria. We obtained and screened full-length versions of the remaining 49 papers. Of these, 43 met at least one exclusion criteria, leaving six. We screened the references of the eligible papers and identified one additional paper that fit our study criteria. We were left with seven studies that estimated the frequency of subsequent TKA among subjects with meniscal damage treated operatively as compared with nonoperatively.

Overview of Selected Studies: The seven studies were published between 2017 and 2023. Salient features of the studies are shown in Table 1, Table 2. Study designs included retrospective cohort studies [14,15], matched case control studies [[16], [17], [18]], and randomized control trials [19,20]. Data sources for these studies included national insurance databases [14,18], institutional databases [16,17], or clinical sources (e.g. medical record, clinical trial databases) [15,19,20]. The studies ranged in sample size, with the largest including over 290,000 participants [14] and the smallest 15 subjects per group [16]. The mean age of the study population ranged from 48.1 years [16] to 67.8 years [15]. All studies had more females than males, ranging from 50.2% [20] to 78.0% [18] females in each study sample. The maximum follow-up time for subjects ranged from five years [15,19,20] to eleven years [16]. Assessment using Joanna Briggs Institute’s critical appraisal tools indicated that all included studies were of acceptable methodological quality for inclusion. While the observational studies and as-treated analyses of the randomized control trials were inherently limited by potential confounding, all studies employed matching, multivariable adjustment, or (in the case of trials) randomization to mitigate confounding. Also, outcome measurement and follow-up rates were generally appropriate across studies.

Risk of TKA: As summarized in Table 3, cumulative incidence of TKA at five years varied widely across studies, as did the differences in TKA incidence between operatively and nonoperatively treated subjects. For studies that only included root tears, the cumulative incidence of TKA at five years in the APM group ranged from 30% [16] to 54.0% [17] while the TKA risk at five years for those in the nonoperative group ranged from 13% [16] to 34.6% [17]. For studies that included all types of tears, the cumulative incidence of TKA at five years in APM-treated subjects ranged from 2.2% [20] to 20% [14] while the TKA risk at five years for nonoperatively treated subjects ranged from 2.9% [20] to 15% [14]. Overall, the between-arm difference (difference in risk of TKA between APM and nonoperative arms) in each study ranged from −0.7% (lower TKA risk in APM recipients) [20] to 17% (higher TKA risk in APM recipients) [16]. The two different methods of ascertaining TKA incidence at 5-years (cumulative incidence or estimation from KM curves) showed similar patterns. All but one study, Noorduyn et al. [20], reported that the APM group had a greater incidence of TKA at five years than the nonoperative group. One study provided age-stratified estimates of the cumulative incidence of TKA at five years between subjects treated with APM vs nonoperative therapy. The excess risk of TKA associated with APM (defined as risk in the APM arm minus risk in the nonoperative arm) was 1.1% in subjects 60–69 years old and 21.5% those ≥70 [15]. No other studies provided stratified TKA rates.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: We reviewed literature on the risk of TKA following operative or nonoperative treatment of meniscal tear. The research protocol was not registered with PROSPERO. We identified seven studies that followed persons with meniscal tears and compared progression to TKA at 5 years among nonoperatively treated subjects to subjects who had APM. Due to the heterogeneity of the sample size, follow up time, data source, and analytic approach, we did not conduct a meta-analysis. Also, as the sample of papers included several very small studies (N ≤ 52) [16,17] and one very large one (N = 290,601) [14], the large study would dominate the results of a meta-analysis, obscuring the findings of the smaller papers. Six of seven studies reported a greater frequency of TKA in subjects receiving APM than in those managed nonoperatively [[14], [15], [16], [17], [18], [19]]. The consistency across the seven studies supports the robustness of the finding of higher frequency of TKA following treatment with APM than treatment with nonoperative therapy.

Studies varied in whether they reported cumulative incidence of TKA and/or TKA-free survival. We abstracted both and found these two approaches gave similar findings, with largely consistent findings across these different methods of TKA ascertainment.

A previous literature review from Winter et al. examined TKA following APM and found an average annual incidence of 2.62% [21]. Our review builds on this prior study by restricting the analysis to papers that (1) confirm all subjects have a documented meniscal tear, and (2) report TKA risks separately for those with meniscal tear treated with APM and those treated nonoperatively, ensuring a control group. This design feature permits our data to be useful for clinicians and patients interested in the comparative likelihood of TKA within five years for each treatment strategy. We found that papers including only root tears reported higher rates of eventual TKA than papers including all forms of meniscal tears, regardless of whether the tears were treated with APM or nonoperatively. Prior studies have shown that root tears often result in meniscal extrusion, disrupting normal knee biomechanics and increasing tibiofemoral joint loading [22]. These factors contribute to increased rate of eventual TKA with root tears as compared with other types of meniscal tears [23].

We note several limitations. Only seven papers met our inclusion criteria. The insurance database studies [14,18], relied on coding and billing information, without accounting for patients lost to follow-up due to changing insurance companies. Also, the open cohort design did not allow for subjects to be followed for consistent periods of time. The cohort studies and the as-treated analysis of the randomized trials are susceptible to residual confounding and confounding by indication. Specifically, the disease characteristics and social determinants influencing selection of APM may overlap with those driving progression of OA and the decision to undergo TKA. Only two out of the seven studies explicitly reported on the number of subjects lost to follow-up [19,20] which could influence the accuracy of our estimates if the losses differed between APM and nonoperatively treated subjects. Similarly, the number of subjects who crossed over from nonoperative therapy to surgery before five years was not recorded consistently, opening the door to potential misclassification. Aside from Bernard et al. [16] and Hernigou et al. [15], none of the other studies provided detailed descriptions of the tear morphology or surgical technique employed for APM. Studies also provided limited information on whether resection of chondral flaps or osteophytes accompanied APM. The sample sizes varied from over 290,000 subjects [14] to just 30 [16]. While length of follow-up varied across studies, each study provided a 5-year TKA risk, permitting us to adopt a standard five-year outcome despite the variable length of follow-up. The studies included in this literature review may not be representative of the wider population of patients with meniscal tear, especially as age, gender, type of meniscal tear, presence and severity of OA, and subject matching differed between studies.

The findings of greater TKA risk following management with APM than with nonoperative therapy has implications for research and practice. From a research standpoint, this finding should prompt additional investigation of the mechanism(s) leading to associations between APM and greater TKA risk. Experimental evidence suggests that partial meniscectomy reduces the load bearing function of the meniscus [24] and imaging data suggest APM may be associated with greater cartilage loss than nonoperative therapy [25]. From a clinical standpoint, patients and their physicians should discuss this increased TKA risk when they discuss whether to treat meniscal tear with APM or nonoperative approaches. For patients who have already undergone APM, these results should not be interpreted as justification for pursuing early TKA. Importantly, while the findings from this analysis suggest an association between APM and subsequent TKA, further research is needed to determine whether any causal relationship exists.

sec.title: Author contributions
sec.p: Conception and design (AH, JK, YC); Analysis (AH, SA, JK, YC); Interpretation of data (AH, SA, YC, JK); Drafting of the article (AH, SA); Critical revision of article (YC, MJ, JK); Obtaining of funding (JK).

sec.title: Role of funding source
sec.p: Supported by: NIH/NIAMS 1R01AR055557.
The funding sources had no role in the study design, collection, analysis and interpretation of the data, writing of the manuscript, or the decision to submit the manuscript for publication.

sec.title: Conflict of interest
sec.p: Dr. Katz reports receiving grants from the Arthritis Foundation and the National Institutes of Health. Dr. Jones reports receiving grants from Pacira and the Arthritis Foundation. Dr. Jones also reports Scientific Advisory Board funding from Grunenthal and Genascence and serves on the Editorial Board of the Orthopedic Journal of Sports Medicine.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: We thank Margaret Broaddus for her editorial assistance.
